name: Project Blue Book
description: Project Blue Book was one of a series of systematic studies of unidentified flying objects (UFOs) conducted by the United States Air Force (USAF). Starting in 1952, it was the third such study, following Project Sign (1947) and Project Grudge (1949). The goal of Project Blue Book, headquartered at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, Ohio, was to determine if UFOs posed a threat to national security, and to scientifically analyze UFO-related data. The project collected over 12,000 UFO reports, concluding that most were misidentified conventional objects or natural phenomena. However, a small percentage remained unexplained. Proponents of UFO theories often cite these unexplained cases as evidence of extraterrestrial visitation. Critics argue that Project Blue Book was designed to debunk UFO sightings and downplay the possibility of extraterrestrial involvement, citing instances of alleged cover-ups and inadequate investigations. The project was officially terminated in December 1969, with the USAF concluding that UFOs posed no threat and further investigation was unwarranted. Despite its official conclusion, Project Blue Book remains a subject of debate and fascination, fueling numerous conspiracy theories and inspiring popular culture depictions of government secrecy surrounding UFOs.
subtitle: USAF's UFO investigation program.

claims.claim: Unexplained UFO sightings represent genuine extraterrestrial encounters.
claims.evidence: A small percentage of Project Blue Book cases remain 'unexplained' after investigation.
claims.criticism: The 'unexplained' status may simply reflect insufficient data or misinterpretation of natural phenomena, rather than definitive proof of extraterrestrial origin. Many cases were poorly documented or investigated.

claims.claim: Project Blue Book was a deliberate cover-up designed to conceal evidence of alien visitation.
claims.evidence: Allegations of manipulated data, suppressed witness testimony, and biased investigations within Project Blue Book.
claims.criticism: The USAF maintains that Project Blue Book was a genuine scientific inquiry, albeit one with limitations in resources and methodology. Concerns about national security during the Cold War may have influenced the approach to UFO investigations.

claims.claim: The Condon Report, commissioned to review Project Blue Book, was biased and flawed.
claims.evidence: Critics argue the Condon Report's conclusions were predetermined and downplayed the significance of unexplained cases. Allegations of bias within the research team.
claims.criticism: The Condon Report, while controversial, was a comprehensive scientific review of UFO evidence, conducted by respected scientists. Its conclusions aligned with the USAF's assessment that UFOs posed no threat.

keyFigures.name: J. Allen Hynek
keyFigures.role: Astronomer and scientific consultant to Project Blue Book; later became a prominent UFO proponent.

keyFigures.name: Edward U. Condon
keyFigures.role: Physicist and head of the Condon Committee, which reviewed Project Blue Book and concluded that further UFO studies were not justified.

timeline.event: Project Sign, the first USAF UFO investigation, is established.
Controversy surrounding the Dexter and Hillsdale, Michigan, sightings.
Project Blue Book is officially terminated.

sources.author: Curtis Peebles
sources.work: Watch the Skies!: A Chronicle of the Flying Saucer Myth
sources.title: Curtis Peebles

sources.author: Daniel Drezner
sources.work: UFOs, the CIA, and the National Security State: An Uncritical History
sources.title: Daniel Drezner
